preFix.title: Yorkshire puddings
preFix.subtitle: Tall, crisp, and hollow in the centre
preFix.excerpt: The classic batter pudding baked in smoking-hot fat until puffed and golden. Twelve individual puddings from a simple batter of flour, eggs, and milk.
preFix.sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Yorkshire pudding entry.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg. Batter pudding method.

content.text: Yorkshire puddings rise on steam and heat: when cold batter meets smoking-hot fat, the liquid turns instantly to steam and the batter puffs up. The result should be tall, with a crisp exterior and a hollow, soft centre.
Two rules govern everything here. The fat must be smoking before the batter goes in. The oven door must not be opened until the puddings are done.

items.name: Duck fat
items.prepNote: or beef dripping, lard, or vegetable oil

content.text: A 12-hole Yorkshire pudding tin gives individual puddings. A 4-hole tin (deeper cups) gives larger ones. Both work; the larger tin needs an extra 5 minutes.
Tip the {{plain-flour}} of flour and the salt into a large bowl. Make a well in the centre and crack in the {{eggs}} eggs. Whisk the eggs into the flour, drawing in the flour from the edges.
Gradually whisk in the {{whole-milk}} of milk until you have a smooth, thin batter. It should have the consistency of single cream.
for at least 30 minutes at room temperature, or up to 24 hours in the fridge.
Heat the oven to 220 degrees C fan. Put a small amount of fat into each hole of the tin (about a teaspoon each). Place the tin in the oven for 10 minutes until the fat is smoking hot.
Working quickly, pour the batter into the tin, filling each hole about two thirds full. Return to the oven immediately and close the door.
Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until puffed and deep golden brown. Do not open the oven before 20 minutes or the puddings will collapse.

items.fix: The fat must be smoking before the batter goes in. Do not open the oven before 20 minutes.
items.cause: The fat was not hot enough, or the oven door was opened too soon.

items.fix: Bake for a full 25 minutes. The puddings should be dark golden brown all over, not pale gold.
items.cause: Underbaked; the structure had not set before they came out of the oven.
items.symptom: Puddings rose then collapsed on taking out of the oven

items.fix: Fill each cup only two thirds full. The batter should be thin enough to pour easily.
items.cause: The batter was too thick, or the tin cups were too full.
items.symptom: Puddings are doughy in the middle

items.fix: Heat the fat for a full 10 minutes at 220 degrees C. You should see it shimmering and beginning to smoke when you open the oven.
items.cause: The fat was only warm, not smoking hot.

content.text: Toad in the hole uses the same batter poured around pork sausages in a large roasting tin. One large pudding (baked in a 30 cm roasting tin) takes about 30 minutes and is served in wedges.

Yorkshire pudding predates the Sunday roast. Hannah Glasse describes it in The Art of Cookery in 1747 as a batter cooked under a roasting spit to catch the dripping. The individual tin version is twentieth century; the original was a single large pudding served before the meat to take the edge off the appetite. Its purpose was economy. Its execution is now a point of honour.
